messages.content_html: there is a default key in T-Deck firmware, let me find it. Everyone on the UK side is using that, we should use the same key for public channel 0 around the world

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: I just checked, this key above izOH6cXN6mrJ5e26oRXNcg== is the key used on the phone client. so if you type this key into a T-Deck you can talk between a BLE companion and a T-Deck on the public channel. You don't need to do anything if you are already running the phone client.
messages.content_html: I just checked, this key above izOH6cXN6mrJ5e26oRXNcg== is the key used on the phone client. so if you type this key into a T-Deck you can talk between a BLE companion and a T-Deck on the public channel. You don't need to do anything if you are already running the phone client.

messages.author: Birch
messages.text: @Uncle Lit if @N0EJ is unable to switch frequencies do you think we should switch PNW over to 915 or is it worth forging ahead with new radios at 910.525?
messages.content_html: @Uncle Lit if @N0EJ is unable to switch frequencies do you think we should switch PNW over to 915 or is it worth forging ahead with new radios at 910.525?

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: The majority of the US users are using 910.525 so far. MeshCore is new so it seems it is easy to change frequency. The best part with MeshCore you can choose to use any of the legal frequencies in the range, unlike Meshtastic. The tradeoff is obviously between freedom to choose for your community or use a common frequency to communicate with others.
messages.content_html: The majority of the US users are using 910.525 so far. MeshCore is new so it seems it is easy to change frequency. The best part with MeshCore you can choose to use any of the legal frequencies in the range, unlike Meshtastic. The tradeoff is obviously between freedom to choose for your community or use a common frequency to communicate with others.

messages.text: if you haven't made a case for one of these nodes before and you have the means, one thing people keep running into is one of the more common filament types likes to melt in heat or the sun, such as when people leave a node in a hot car—I can't quote the types that don't do this but accounting for that (unless you already did) will give your design a leg up in the durability dept

messages.author: supernovasky
messages.text: I appreciate the persistence required to keep trying to make something work, but only up until the sunk cost fallacy takes over as the driving factor in that persistence. If you can't regroup and head toward something with more promise, then did you really want your efforts to bear fruit in the first place? Or is it that you just can't stop yourself at that point? Saying this here, so I don't say it somewhere else

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: I am, uh, new to all this and don't know the people involved, want to keep up but probably rather not. HOWEVER, can somebody answer this? Is it true that: (1) MQTT is the technology that lets you bridge comms thru the Internet in addition to wireless, and (2) Meshtastic has chosen to implement MQTT, Meshcore has chosen not to? And if all that is correct, is there a point being made that any perception that Meshtastic is more capable than Meshcore is skewed, because people are not recognizing that Internet connectivity is influencing the results? Do I have the gist of this...disagreement...right?

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Yep. I was initially excited by Meshtastic, but Meshcore launched about two weeks later, and it was immediately apparent that it has actual network design baked in. Meshtastic really just seems like a useful proof-of-concept that got enthusiastically adopted, and now needs to be enthusiastically left behind in favor of a protocol that's designed to actually work in the real world
messages.content_html: Yep. I was initially excited by Meshtastic, but Meshcore launched about two weeks later, and it was immediately apparent that it has actual network design baked in. Meshtastic really just seems like a useful proof-of-concept that got enthusiastically adopted, and now needs to be enthusiastically left behind in favor of a protocol that's designed to actually work in the real world

messages.author: Dan - WI7FI [PNW]
messages.text: the primary conflict is that real world testing has shown that meshtastic has basically never been what you could consider "reliable" in any deployment that I've heard of (with over a year of time to do so), while meshcore has been deployed in many different places, and has shown much better reliability than meshtastic in almost every case, especially for direct messages (in just a few months)
messages.content_html: the primary conflict is that real world testing has shown that meshtastic has basically never been what you could consider "reliable" in any deployment that I've heard of (with over a year of time to do so), while meshcore has been deployed in many different places, and has shown much better reliability than meshtastic in almost every case, especially for direct messages (in just a few months)
messages.reactions_html: 3
messages.reply_html:
Pete
For myself: I'm not all that interested in the drama for the drama sake, but I AM interested in better understanding how to use the technology, so I'd like to understand the general shape of whatever conflict there is. I appreciate what Meshtastic has done as a proof-of-concept, but I'm interested in emergency comms that are reliable and permit a reasonable level of privacy via encryption and lack of excess repeats. Meshcore seems really promising, and vastly more capable than Meshtastic in that regard. I'm happy to be part of communities working to get enough saturation of Meshcore in Portland that it can be reliable and enduring, and regional connectivity would be super cool too.

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: In Meshtastic every node by default repeats. As your local mesh scales up with more nodes, they are a lot of packets flying every which way every tube one node sends something out. Usually are the telemetry packets from all of them, drowning your text messages that you care

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: One consequence of this, that I think is underappreciated: If there ever was a catastrophic event (earthquake, wildfire etc) that knocks out electric or Internet or cell towers etc, imagine how the network would look if everybody who fooled around with a device in the last few years took one out of the closet, dusted it off, and set it up. Meshtastic, I think, would be totally useless. Meshcore might or might not suffer a bit at first, but it would be pretty obvious that oversaturation of repeaters can cause problems, and lots of clients is not such a big deal. It would be relatively easy for people to start taking repeaters offline and experiment in real time, without sacrificing their own connectivity via client nodes. (edited)

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: I am hopeful that once we can built up sufficient (and well-laid out) saturation -- that is, once we have enough well-placed repeaters in a given area -- newcomers generally won't have much reason to set up a repeater of their own. They can just set up a node, and possibly a room server for their group of friends, unless they actually find they need a repeater.It seems like there'd be a tipping point, maybe it takes real world experimentation to figure out where that tipping point is....

messages.reply_html:
dit dit
This is sort of common sense, but it is also important that repeaters don't move. The Meshtastic mesh changes whenever someone moves their nodes - even just a foot on a table inside their apartment/house can have a big impact. With MeshCore, a static repeater can be placed in a reliable spot for anyone to use no matter where anybody takes their companion node. (edited)

messages.author: recrof
messages.text: working mesh pro tip: 1.7.0 repeaters/room servers have default interference threshold( set int.thresh ) set to noise_floor + 14, which can be hit easily in noisy areas.. I already needed to set it to 0 because of that. if your repeater all of the sudden refuses to repeat, disable the feature.
messages.content_html: working mesh pro tip: 1.7.0 repeaters/room servers have default interference threshold(set int.thresh) set to noise_floor + 14, which can be hit easily in noisy areas.. I already needed to set it to 0 because of that. if your repeater all of the sudden refuses to repeat, disable the feature.

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Mere mortal here, hoping for a word of wisdom from the wise radio wizards here. It seems to connect to what's been discussed here in the last couple days, but I don't know the tech well enough to follow that discussion well. In SE Portland we have a little cluster of four people in close proximity, with access to at least five locations. We have four rooftop antennae up so far, and I think it's fair to say we have a good understanding of the basics of Meshcore/LoRa. Our problem is a ridge that goes between us; we're divided two on one side, two on the other. We have put our antennae as high as practical, and are still considering knocking on a couple doors up on the hill. However, despite what the survey says, we have intermittent connectivity without an intermediate hop! The question is: If we test and determine that, for instance, a lower spread factor or more redundant coding rate improves connectivity across the ridge, is there a way to establish a link with those settings, and bridge it to the "standard" network settings? What kind of equipment would we need for a LoRa-LoRa bridge (as opposed to LoRa-ESP32)? Tagging @Woodward , @tedder , @WanderBuddy (edited)

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Well, we've got the antennae up pretty high, and with some placements as we've moved things around we've seen good SNR numbers. I'm curious about this question regardless of whether or not it provides a solution for this particular problem, though -- is it possible to bridge one set of LoRa settings to another, to optimize a single connection for its conditions (going thru lots of trees, going extra long distance, etc.)?
messages.content_html: Well, we've got the antennae up pretty high, and with some placements as we've moved things around we've seen good SNR numbers. I'm curious about this question regardless of whether or not it provides a solution for this particular problem, though -- is it possible to bridge one set of LoRa settings to another, to optimize a single connection for its conditions (going thru lots of trees, going extra long distance, etc.)?

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Near Atkinson Elementary to near 50th and Hawthorne. I used a mapping tool at one point (wish I saved the link) and I believe it said we needed something like 30 feet on each end (height on my end is more beneficial) to clear the ridge. I don't think we've measured the height on either end, but I'd guess they're both high 20s, and more or less maxed out to our comfort level without major construction. (Me and one of the folks over there.) Messages have been relayed multiple times between my place and both of those on the other side, but it's not reliable. It's possible one of them is just a setup thing, and is actually gonna work fine once reconfigured. My repeater consistently reports positive SNR to that one. The other, it's as bad as something like -17 SNR, but has been occasionally functional. (edited)

it's possible with esp32 devices and UDP. You can bridge between presets with that -- that said, if you pick presets that have drastically different performance, one of them will bottleneck

messages.author: Woodward
messages.text: It can help with packet collision in saturated meshes, by reducing the number of nodes using the Lora frequencies. Short distances from clients to repeaters makes sense to use the 2.4 spectrum, leaving the 915 for repeaters only.

messages.author: mattsains
messages.text: Meshcore capture the flag Hello everyone! This Sunday morning, June 22, I will hide a meshcore node somewhere near Ballard. I’ll post here when and roughly where I’ve placed it. After I send the rough location, first to find the node and send the capture information (see below) will win a $10 amazon gift card. There is a 15 minute time advantage for anyone who arrives via public transport, walking, or by bicycle - let me know if you’ve found the node without using a car and I’ll apply this advantage to your time (honour system). The game will end at 2pm The node name is mattsains2. It has custom programming on it to enable this capture the flag game. Sending an advert will trigger the node into sending its own zero-hop advert. It is required that you send an advert and receive an advert from the node in order to communicate with the node Sending a direct (zero hop) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this. TDeck nodes are not recommended, because the messages are too long to display due to this bug: https://github.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/issues/238 Rules: Please return the node back to exactly where you find it, so that others can participate. Stealing the node will result in seven years of bad luck. Be careful not to arouse the suspicion of muggles who may want to see what you’re searching for - be discreet. Direction-finding equipment is allowed but not necessary. Trying to hack the node will probably just break it and ruin the fun, so don’t bother. This is my own event and doesn't have anything to do with this Discord server. This is the first time I've organised such an event so it's possible it will all fall apart and be disappointing, please prepare yourself for that. (edited)
messages.content_html:
Meshcore capture the flag

Hello everyone! This Sunday morning, June 22, I will hide a meshcore node somewhere near Ballard. I’ll post here when and roughly where I’ve placed it. After I send the rough location, first to find the node and send the capture information (see below) will win a $10 amazon gift card. There is a 15 minute time advantage for anyone who arrives via public transport, walking, or by bicycle - let me know if you’ve found the node without using a car and I’ll apply this advantage to your time (honour system). The game will end at 2pm
  • The node name is mattsains2. It has custom programming on it to enable this capture the flag game.
  • Sending an advert will trigger the node into sending its own zero-hop advert. It is required that you send an advert and receive an advert from the node in order to communicate with the node
  • Sending a direct (zero hop) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this.
  • TDeck nodes are not recommended, because the messages are too long to display due to this bug: https://github.com/ripplebiz/MeshCore/issues/238
Rules:
  • Please return the node back to exactly where you find it, so that others can participate. Stealing the node will result in seven years of bad luck.
  • Be careful not to arouse the suspicion of muggles who may want to see what you’re searching for - be discreet.
  • Direction-finding equipment is allowed but not necessary.
  • Trying to hack the node will probably just break it and ruin the fun, so don’t bother.
  • This is my own event and doesn't have anything to do with this Discord server.
  • This is the first time I've organised such an event so it's possible it will all fall apart and be disappointing, please prepare yourself for that.
 (edited)

messages.author: mattsains
messages.text: Meshcore capture the flag - event ended Hello everyone! I have hidden a meshcore node somewhere near coordinates 47.66644, -122.3708 W. First to find the node and send the capture information (see below) will win a $10 amazon gift card. There is a 15 minute time advantage for anyone who arrives via public transport, walking, or by bicycle - let me know if you’ve found the node without using a car and I’ll apply this advantage to your time (honour system). The game will end at 3pm The node name is mattsains2. It has custom programming on it to enable this capture the flag game. Sending an advert will trigger the node into sending its own zero-hop advert. It is required that you send an advert and receive an advert from the node in order to communicate with the node Sending a zero hop (not flooded or routed) message will cause it to reply with a hint of its location, as well as instructions on how to win the game. If you send a multi-hop message, you will not receive this hint but you will get a different reply explaining this. Rules: Please return the node back to exactly where you find it, so that others can participate. Stealing the node will result in seven years of bad luck. Be careful not to arouse the suspicion of muggles who may want to see what you’re searching for - be discreet. Direction-finding equipment is allowed but not necessary. Trying to hack the node will probably just break it and ruin the fun, so don’t bother. This is my own event and doesn’t have anything to do with this Discord server. This is the first time I've organised such an event so it's possible it will all fall apart and be disappointing, please prepare yourself for that. (edited)

messages.text: The thing that kills me about WhatsApp, is while the e2e encryption is good, for some reason people disregard that it requires you to merge your contacts, and all that data gets shared with you know who. messages.author: Pete
messages.text: As a Portlander, I'm very interested to see what happens if you set up some Meshcore infrastructure. Followed a bit of discussion with you on the Willamette Valley channel recently, looked to me like you were maybe separated by a ridge or something from the most promising long distance repeater...so, probably some further troubleshooting or infrastructrure to address. But it feels like we're on the cusp of making a Portland-Salem connection (there have been sightings from one to the other), and I would think something where you're at would hasten that outcome.
messages.content_html: As a Portlander, I'm very interested to see what happens if you set up some Meshcore infrastructure. Followed a bit of discussion with you on the Willamette Valley channel recently, looked to me like you were maybe separated by a ridge or something from the most promising long distance repeater...so, probably some further troubleshooting or infrastructrure to address. But it feels like we're on the cusp of making a Portland-Salem connection (there have been sightings from one to the other), and I would think something where you're at would hasten that outcome.
messages.reply_html:
Torg_OR (Don)
but that is the question I suppose - unless I get another repeater to set up ...

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Yeah, I figured it was probably all thru one repeater on each end. Shame there's not a way to expose the path from something like that. Though, per Andy Kirby's latest video, it seems the packets contain the info, just most of the software doesn't expose it yet?
messages.content_html: Yeah, I figured it was probably all thru one repeater on each end. Shame there's not a way to expose the path from something like that. Though, per Andy Kirby's latest video, it seems the packets contain the info, just most of the software doesn't expose it yet?

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: It seems to me like receiving-but-can't-send is a common issue until you get a good position and/or an upgraded antenna. Based on my experience and what I hear from others. I don't have that issue from home much these days, where I have a good 5.8dBi antenna on the roof. But when I'm at my partner's place with a pocket heltec indoors, I often receive multiple "public channel" messages, but can't seem to get anything out.
messages.content_html: It seems to me like receiving-but-can't-send is a common issue until you get a good position and/or an upgraded antenna. Based on my experience and what I hear from others. I don't have that issue from home much these days, where I have a good 5.8dBi antenna on the roof. But when I'm at my partner's place with a pocket heltec indoors, I often receive multiple "public channel" messages, but can't seem to get anything out.

Advanced Meshtastic - LoRa Configuration
messages.reactions_html: 1
messages.reply_html:
mcarper
Long story short is you can fit a narrow bandwidth frequency range into a congested band. So the most narrow bandwidth that MC supports is 62.5khz. Then you adjust your SF to make sure it's not super slow

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Cool, I see, the proposed settings are very different from the ones in any of the MT presets discussed in that video. It has bandwidth (62.5) that's only used in the "Very Long/Slow" preset, and spreading factor (7) that's only used in the "Short/Fast" preset. Interesting that settings so far off the default would be expected to yield better results!
messages.content_html: Cool, I see, the proposed settings are very different from the ones in any of the MT presets discussed in that video. It has bandwidth (62.5) that's only used in the "Very Long/Slow" preset, and spreading factor (7) that's only used in the "Short/Fast" preset. Interesting that settings so far off the default would be expected to yield better results!
messages.reactions_html: 1
messages.reply_html:
mcarper
That's one of the best videos I've seen on what the different configurations do. One thing to note is the coding rate doesn't have to match with other radios. So on a pocket node setting it to 4/8 makes sense but for a rooftop 4/5 makes more sense unless you're right on the edge of being heard then bump it up until you have a good link to the rest of the network.

messages.author: Pete
messages.text: Well yeah, I'm trusting that part will come together at least for a test Just wanna know how to participate most effectively if/when it does. Thanks for the point about CF being something you can choose independently of the rest of the network though -- I hadn't realized that!
messages.content_html: Well yeah, I'm trusting that part will come together at least for a test Just wanna know how to participate most effectively if/when it does. Thanks for the point about CF being something you can choose independently of the rest of the network though -- I hadn't realized that!

mcarper

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: with all the Station G2 talks, I want to add that the Nano G2 is also very good. I think it is the best EDC device, after I printed an enclosure for it to add a 3000mAh battery. I received it on Friday. Charged it over night. Starting using it on Saturday. Today is Wednesday, 4 days later. It is still on the same charge. app says it is at 38% 3.46V. I have GPS turned off. I has a hardwired physical power switch to turn it off. also hardwired switch for power and buzzer. The Nano G2 internal antenna is just as good as a RAK with a good antenna when both are sitting on a table. The Nano G2's internal antenna is supposed to still work well when close to a body. It does seem to receive OK about a 1 mile away with the device on my body.

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: I would probably opt for the app to have suggested presets per region, for example what already exists: Australia New Zealand USA etc etc. And then another section for "Community Meshes" that have a GPS location or geo fence that can be visualised on a map overlay. Main use cases I can think of, are probably: I just want to tinker and use this in my country I want to join a community mesh, that might be using some obscure settings I want to setup a private mesh on a frequency away from others For the user experience, in the app, you'd connect to a new device, and it would ask for your display name, and then ask you to pick radio settings. I'd have the UI setup where you can: Pick a default suggestion for your country/region or, search from a list of mesh communities, like "Puget Mesh", "SoCal Mesh" or "GizzyMesh" etc or enter custom radio settings if you want something custom I think the colouring system might end up like the LongFast, MediumShort thing, where you don't quite know what they mean without looking at some table somewhere, but it's been great to get discussion rolling
messages.content_html:
I would probably opt for the app to have suggested presets per region, for example what already exists:
  • Australia
  • New Zealand
  • USA etc etc. And then another section for "Community Meshes" that have a GPS location or geo fence that can be visualised on a map overlay. Main use cases I can think of, are probably:
  • I just want to tinker and use this in my country
  • I want to join a community mesh, that might be using some obscure settings
  • I want to setup a private mesh on a frequency away from others For the user experience, in the app, you'd connect to a new device, and it would ask for your display name, and then ask you to pick radio settings. I'd have the UI setup where you can:
  • Pick a default suggestion for your country/region
  • or, search from a list of mesh communities, like "Puget Mesh", "SoCal Mesh" or "GizzyMesh" etc
  • or enter custom radio settings if you want something custom I think the colouring system might end up like the LongFast, MediumShort thing, where you don't quite know what they mean without looking at some table somewhere, but it's been great to get discussion rolling
messages.reactions_html: 5

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: Can call the community entries whatever you want, but if they collide with other names from other countries/regions, they'd need to be nested under some sort of group name, such as the country or region otherwise users wouldn't know what it's for or related to. Ie, SoCal Mesh, has no meaning in New Zealand, so if I saw that as an option I'd have no idea if I should select it or not. But I think the geo zoning would be a good option. Just look at the map and pick from meshes in your area.

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: The mobile app uploads internet map nodes to my server, which then forwards to recrofs map server. The node info being uploaded is signed by your companion key, which allows us to mark it as being uploaded by you. In which case when you try to delete a node, this is also signed by your key, and allowed if you were the one that uploaded it. My API server also prevents you from uploading companion nodes that are not you. (edited)
messages.content_html: The mobile app uploads internet map nodes to my server, which then forwards to recrofs map server. The node info being uploaded is signed by your companion key, which allows us to mark it as being uploaded by you. In which case when you try to delete a node, this is also signed by your key, and allowed if you were the one that uploaded it. My API server also prevents you from uploading companion nodes that are not you. (edited)

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: If you're using the mobile app, you can use the 3dot menu on the contact list, select share, and then tap internet map.
messages.content_html: If you're using the mobile app, you can use the 3dot menu on the contact list, select share, and then tap internet map.
messages.reply_html:
Wavesonics
hey how do I add a repeater/room server to the internet map?

messages.author: Wavesonics
messages.text: ah nice, that does it. I feel like it would be a nice option to also have in the Remote Management screen, like nets to the Set position menu item
messages.content_html: ah nice, that does it. I feel like it would be a nice option to also have in the Remote Management screen, like nets to the Set position menu item

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: Yeah would be nice, but the upload to map functionality has to use the advert data from your contacts. So if you set position via remote admin, don't send/receive an advert, and then upload the repeater to internet map (ie from the new button you suggest), it won't actually upload with the position you just set. Having the button on the contacts list makes it more connected to the fact that you're sharing the current state of the data from your contacts list
messages.content_html: Yeah would be nice, but the upload to map functionality has to use the advert data from your contacts. So if you set position via remote admin, don't send/receive an advert, and then upload the repeater to internet map (ie from the new button you suggest), it won't actually upload with the position you just set. Having the button on the contacts list makes it more connected to the fact that you're sharing the current state of the data from your contacts list
messages.reply_html:
Wavesonics
ah nice, that does it. I feel like it would be a nice option to also have in the Remote Management screen, like nets to the Set position menu item

messages.author: Panic
messages.text: My philosophy is that the prohibition of encryption is a good thing to make sure the resources aren't secretly clogged up with commercial use-cases. However, the packet-switched nature of the IP network means that many people can share the resources at the same time, so you're not preventing someone from communicating by sending garbage youself.

messages.author: nullrouten
messages.text: Most valleys have Routers in 3 directions … makes everyone have 3 chances to send/rx packets (they just need a view of any hills ,,,, not “the one hill”…. )… redundancy fixed the mesh. We know because everyone is logging and the more routers we deployed the more of the mesh was reachable Client is fundamentally broken (black hole problem), so we worked around it. It moved airtime from clients To those “best 7%” of any 10 mile area….

messages.author: nullrouten
messages.text: We had 650 nodes happy on MS, then deployed MF and 530 moved…. (Everything works even better now)… has been humming the whole time … now 0-hop made conversations better (thank you)… because we don’t run out of hops “sometimes”. Now we hit the edges so nobody is left out. Going to be interesting as we roll MC now… thanks to lower background noise and more hops. And if you think about it… MC basically encourages repeating …. It’s doing what we did with MT a year ago (just deploy routers whenever you need them). Hops was another fun meme. Scolded for going over 3… until we got a dozen people on 7 and “oh gee we can talk now”. Now we’re easily hitting 9-11 thanks to 0hop (so I guess exceeding 3 wasn’t so horrible after all). It wasn’t without resistance … I got scolded until I had 2 - 3 routers on hills and people then discovered the mesh started working and they could chat.. then we just built out across the land . Luckily both those taboo things are gone with MC

messages.text: I'm bummed I missed the Meshcore meetup in Tacoma today, but had a great time at SeaGL ! messages.author: The Mad Codger
messages.text: The major problem—one of the major problems, for there are several—one of the many major problems with governing people is that of whom you get to do it; or rather of who manages to get people to let them do it to them. To summarize: it is a well-known fact that those people who must want to rule people are, ipso facto, those least suited to do it. To summarize the summary: anyone who is capable of getting themselves made President should on no account be allowed to do the job. (edited)

messages.author: BrianEnigma
messages.text: I'm not sure where to ask this or even 100% what I'm asking. I'm in Portland and I can definitely see a lot of conversation happening in the Public and Bot channels that appear to be coming from Seattle. But it seems like my messages aren't making it up there. I can't interact with the HowlBot in the bot channel, for instance. Does it even make sense to have a mesh that only goes one direction? Could I have something misconfigured? Might there be a bad node between here and there? Mostly I'm confused and not sure how I'd even begin troubleshooting pathing.

messages.author: Jade
messages.text: The messages are coming through 66 - Goat Mountain. When it was originally put up messages would travel both ways, however, something happened probably due to weather where you're only getting them one way now. in the spring, it will be upgraded and also raised higher.. It was also misaligned due to a collision with another repeater with the same prefix. (edited)
messages.content_html: The messages are coming through 66 - Goat Mountain. messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: my opinion is that bridging is not going to work well. the reason being, for a bridge to work, you need to have repeaters at a spot that can see both meshes. you need two repeaters, and some other glue in between. That mean it is a lot harder to run with solar. without solar that means the spots you can have a bridging setup are even selective. If the meshes are bridged successfully and works close to 100% of the time, you will then double each mesh's utilization. If one of the meshes is already very busy, you will start having more and more collisions. This is just me theory crafting. If you see anything wrong with my assumptions, please point them out.
messages.content_html: my opinion is that bridging is not going to work well. the reason being, for a bridge to work, you need to have repeaters at a spot that can see both meshes. you need two repeaters, and some other glue in between. messages.author: nullrouten
messages.text: , we need a way to discover a route (if we’re going to block a Flood) , mesh becomes useless (unless you know exactly which path). Maybe the entire topology can be downloaded offline for companions to ingest. Maybe we can learn from OSPF, EIGRP , BGP, and RIP . Or perhaps the network acts a tiny bit like BitTorrent in disseminating topology for people so they never need to flood. Flood is actually a great way to scale a message to many listeners…. But it’s not really the Flood that’s the problem. It’s people using it to discover where they need to go ….or chatting to the whole public mesh…. If we didn’t need to use Flood, then it wouldn’t be such a problem.

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: I don't feel like arguing about this, but the point is this: If one protocol has an MTU larger than the other, you have to fragment the packets. There is no other way. The only penalty you're paying is the additional packet's header
messages.content_html: I don't feel like arguing about this, but the point is this: If one protocol has an MTU larger than the other, you have to fragment the packets. There is no other way. The only penalty you're paying is the additional packet's header

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: you can always do that if you want with your protocol. But, it sure seems like reticulum was designed before lora was a goal, and the MTU was set at 512. Just throwing up hands and saying you'll never support it seems very silly. Sending two packets is a perfectly reasonable way to handle sending more data. And it would be transparant because rnode would re-assemble, no? If it's not transparently reassembling, it's a bug, IMO
messages.content_html: you can always do that if you want with your protocol. But, it sure seems like reticulum was designed before lora was a goal, and the MTU was set at 512. And it would be transparant because rnode would re-assemble, no? If it's not transparently reassembling, it's a bug, IMO

messages.author: Symbioquine
messages.text: It's surprising to me that there isn't (at least as far as I can tell) some sort of basic throttling/de-duplicating going on... I see two issues related to it, but apparently not much traction: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1223 https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1305 It seems obvious that some mechanism to drop spurious/noisy advert repeats will be needed as meshes get very large. Additionally, any protection should be "defensive" - in other words, it should let the network protect itself from (FCC compliant) but misbehaving nodes. (As opposed to relying on good behavior from all nodes.) The next question is: How would that need to work? Issue #1223 explores this a bit, but with the presupposition that it should be probabilistic and without describing the chain of reasoning that leads to that idea. I suspect it is a matter of whether deterministic throttling could be achieved with the small(-ish) memory available on many of the repeater microcontrollers. I'm curious whether that analysis has actually been done and what other strategies have been considered? Some others that I can imagine: Maintain a small local in-memory table of most recent adverts and only repeat those which haven't been heard recently (Simplest and maybe avoids at least the worst spam cases) Replace certain key repeaters with fancier hardware (like Raspberry Pis) that have enough memory to track multiple days of all the adverts heard and - like above - only repeat those which meet the throttling criteria (e.g. greater than 24h) Implement a gossip protocol that utilizes idle radio time to build a distributed list of the noisiest nodes so that cooperating repeaters can consistently rate throttle adverts (and even other messages) from those nodes. (Probably lots of other strategies that's just off the top of my head...)
messages.content_html:
It's surprising to me that there isn't (at least as far as I can tell) some sort of basic throttling/de-duplicating going on... I see two issues related to it, but apparently not much traction:
  • https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1223
  • https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/issues/1305
It seems obvious that some mechanism to drop spurious/noisy advert repeats will be needed as meshes get very large. Additionally, any protection should be "defensive" - in other words, it should let the network protect itself from (FCC compliant) but misbehaving nodes. (As opposed to relying on good behavior from all nodes.) The next question is: How would that need to work? Issue #1223 explores this a bit, but with the presupposition that it should be probabilistic and without describing the chain of reasoning that leads to that idea. I suspect it is a matter of whether deterministic throttling could be achieved with the small(-ish) memory available on many of the repeater microcontrollers. I'm curious whether that analysis has actually been done and what other strategies have been considered? Some others that I can imagine:
  • Maintain a small local in-memory table of most recent adverts and only repeat those which haven't been heard recently (Simplest and maybe avoids at least the worst spam cases)
  • Replace certain key repeaters with fancier hardware (like Raspberry Pis) that have enough memory to track multiple days of all the adverts heard and - like above - only repeat those which meet the throttling criteria (e.g. greater than 24h)
  • Implement a gossip protocol that utilizes idle radio time to build a distributed list of the noisiest nodes so that cooperating repeaters can consistently rate throttle adverts (and even other messages) from those nodes.
  • (Probably lots of other strategies that's just off the top of my head...)

messages.author: Symbioquine
messages.text: If the data in adverts is useful and going to solve for a real mesh user story (other than "this is cool to have an automatic map/contact-list"), I believe the mesh software would need to be smarter about how to propagate that information - probably a gossip protocol that uses lower airtime moments to share information about what nodes are online and how recently they've been seen. Such a protocol could be pretty efficient airtime-wise because most of the information in adverts doesn't need to be repeated every time and the "fidelity of the information communicated could be spatially attenuated" (I'll try to explain what I mean by this in the next message). In my mind only the stable portions of the mesh - repeaters/rooms - participate in that gossip protocol. Companions then query their nearby mesh to get the information they need.
messages.content_html: If the data in adverts is useful and going to solve for a real mesh user story (other than "this is cool to have an automatic map/contact-list"), I believe the mesh software would need to be smarter about how to propagate that information - probably a gossip protocol that uses lower airtime moments to share information about what nodes are online and how recently they've been seen. Such a protocol could be pretty efficient airtime-wise because most of the information in adverts doesn't need to be repeated every time and the "fidelity of the information communicated could be spatially attenuated" (I'll try to explain what I mean by this in the next message). In my mind only the stable portions of the mesh - repeaters/rooms - participate in that gossip protocol. Companions then query their nearby mesh to get the information they need.
messages.reply_html:
🌲 Tree
Ultimately that would not solve the finite airtime problem, and as utilization continued to grow (even without adverts), eventually the odds of an advert being successful drops to the point where that once every 2 week checkin may not get repeated at all, or very broadly.

messages.author: alj
messages.text: Please see if this logic is correct: 1. for repeaters 3 bytes might be the way to go, they don't flood often (hopefully!) but you don't want any even low probability collisions to stand in the way of connecting to your repeater for management purposes. 2. for companion devices 2 byte may be the way to go because in-transit hash size depends on a sender settings, i e "3 byte" repeater will append 2 byte path if sender had that in settings.... (edited)

messages.text: I think (also based on example-repeater code) when you set default region and that region is absent in a region map of a repeater (which is how 99.99% of all repeaters configured now) it does not fall back to a wildcard region.
